Peshwa Baji Rao 1

[Maratha History](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/maratha-history/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Peshwa Baji Rao 1

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Aug 18, 2023

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

4

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

5

Share

The Peshwa era marks a fascinating transformation in the Maratha Empire—from a warrior confederacy under the Chhatrapati to a more centralized, Brahmin-led administrative and military power. The period from Balaji Vishwanath to Raghunath Rao is not just politically significant, but symbolically rich, filled with ambition, betrayal, reform, and resilience.

Technically speaking Moropant Pingle, Shivaji’s revenue minister, was the first ever Peshwa, though Shivaji referred to him as Pant Pradhan. The rise of the Peshwa was more an effect of the internal conflict between Tara Bai and Shahu, and the latter’s victory saw him appointing Balaji Vishwanath as the Peshwa.

It also marked the ascendancy of the Chitpavan Brahmins, Vishwanath played a major role not just in Shahu’s victory over Tarabai, but also the battles against Aurangzeb, that left the Mughal emperor totally broken and dispirited. And later on supported the Sayyid brothers in deposing the Mughal emperor Farrukshiyar.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!eIz_!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbe11baa6-984c-400f-ae30-c7a82db4e485_640x480.jpeg)

![br](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!eIz_!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbe11baa6-984c-400f-ae30-c7a82db4e485_640x480.jpeg "br")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Early Days**

Baji Rao, was born on August 18, 1700 into the Chitpavan Brahmin Bhat family, already elevated by Balaji Vishwanath’s brilliance at Saswad, growing up amidst war councils and battlefield maps, he accompanied his father on campaigns, absorbing tactics, terrain, and the pulse of the empire.

In 1720, Balaji Vishwanath passed away. Shahu, recognizing Baji Rao’s fire, appointed him Peshwa despite opposition from older courtiers.His youth was seen as a risk, but it would became the Maratha Empire’s greatest asset. Baji Rao’s younger brother, Chimaji Appa, was no less formidable. He led the legendary campaign against the Portuguese in the Konkan and captured *Vasai Fort* in 1739. His military acumen complemented Baji Rao’s northern conquests.

**Anant Ram Sumant** and **Shripatrao Pratinidhi** were seasoned administrators, deeply entrenched in Shahu’s court. Both had seniority, experience, and influence, and saw Baji Rao’s appointment as a breach of protoco as well as reflecting a broader discomfort with generational change and the rise of the Chitpavan Brahmins.

**Rise to Power**

Baji Rao, hit back, by appointing younger people, like Malhar Rao Holkar, Ranoji Shinde, the Pawar Brothers, most of them barely out of their teens. More important they were not from the powerful Deshmukh clans, that traditionally dominated the Maratha court. Holkar was a Dhangar, Ranoji Shinde was a village Patil, the Pawar brothers were Marathas not related to the Deshmukh clans.

On the other hand, the once formidable reach of the Maratha Empire began to fray at its edges. The Siddis of Janjira held firm control over the Konkan coast, defying Maratha naval ambitions. In the newly annexed provinces of Malwa and Gujarat, local nobles carved out autonomous fiefdoms, answering to no central authority. Meanwhile, Asaf Jah I, a former Mughal governor, severed ties.

And then came the rift within the descendants of Shivaji Maharaj, as Sambhaji II of Kolhapur staked his claim to the title of Chhatrapati, challenging Shahu of Satara. The Maratha polity, once united by sword and sacrifice, now splintered into rival courts. This internal discord played directly into the hands of Nizam-ul-Mulk, who watched the Deccan with calculating eyes.

> *“Let us transcend the barren Deccan and conquer central India. The
> Mughals have become weak, insolent, womanizers and opium-addicts. It
> is time to drive from the holy land of Bharatvarsha the outcastes and
> the barbarians. Let us throw them back over the Himalayas. Strike,
> strike at the trunk and the branches will fall off themselves. Listen
> and I shall plant the saffron flag on the walls of Attock.”*

Amidst this fragmentation, Baji Rao rose, not merely as a warrior, but as a strategist who believed that only a Hindu *Pad Padshahi,* could transcend these petty rivalries. For that, the Maratha Empire had to break free from the confines of the Deccan and carve its destiny across Bharat. With the Mughal Empire shrinking and in terminal decline, he felt it was the right time for the Marathas to occupy the space.

Most Maratha Sardars balked at Baji Rao’s audacious vision of expansion beyond the Deccan, still caught in a regionalist mindset. But Shahu, the Chhatrapati of Satara, saw something in Baji Rao, a fire, a clarity, a civilizational hunge, as he gave his quiet nod.

Thus began a twenty-year campaign that would shake the foundations of Hindustan. Baji Rao I, with unmatched speed and daring, struck terror into the hearts of his enemies. His cavalry became legend, as the Maratha banner flew from Bundelkhand to Delhi.

**Campaign against the Nizam**

On January 4, 1721, Baji Rao met the Nizam at Chikalthana, seeking peace in a land already fraying at the seams. In a gesture of statesmanship, he even supported the Nizam’s revolt against the Mughal emperor, believing that a shared Deccan sovereignty could be forged.

But the Nizam had other plans. Once his position was secure, he turned on the Marathas with ruthless precision, expelling their revenue collectors and exploiting the rift between Shahu and Sambhaji II. Refusing to pay *chauth* or *sardeshmukh*, he challenged the very legitimacy of Maratha authority.

As tensions deepened, old rivals , Parashuram Pratinidhi and Chandrasen Jadhav, threw their weight behind the Nizam and Sambhaji II, opposing Shahu and undermining Maratha unity. Seizing the moment, the Nizam marched into Pune and installed Sambhaji II as Chhatrapati, a direct affront to Shahu’s legitimacy.

But Baji Rao was not one to be cornered as he struck back in a masterclass of mobile warfare with lightning speed. On February 28, 1728, at the Battle of Palkhed, he routed the Nizam, his cavalry weaving through terrain with such precision leaving the Nizam disoriented and defeated. It wasn’t just a victory, it was a declaration that the Maratha Empire would not be fractured, nor outmaneuvered.

Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery, in his book *A Concise History of Warfare*, praised Baji Rao I’s campaign at Palkhed as a “masterpiece of strategic mobility.” He admired how Baji Rao’s cavalry, unburdened by artillery or supply lines, moved with astonishing speed and precision—living off the land and bewildering the Nizam’s forces.

Montgomery went further, comparing Baji Rao’s tactics to those of General William Tecumseh Sherman during the American Civil War. Sherman’s famed “March to the Sea” involved rapid, deep penetration into enemy territory without traditional supply lines, relying on foraging and psychological warfare to break Confederate resistance. Montgomery saw in Baji Rao a precursor to this modern maneuver warfare, light, fast, and devastatingly effective

In the aftermath of relentless campaigns, the Nizam was compelled to sue for peace. With this, a seismic shift occurred, he formally recognized **Shahu Maharaj** as the rightful **Chhatrapati**, and conceded the Marathas’ sovereign right to collect **chauth and sardeshmukhi** across the Deccan.

**Foundation of Pune**

In 1728, **Baji Rao I**, moved his base to **Pune the** a quiet town poised to become the beating heart of Maratha resurgence, laying the foundations of a city that would echo with valor, intellect, and reform for centuries to come. Construction of the **Shaniwar Wada** began under his stewardship, a citadel not just of stone, but of spirit. It would become the seat of Maratha power, a crucible of strategy and sacrifice, and a silent witness to the rise and trials of a great empire

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Kj9x!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9138c02e-db70-4952-af3b-39a11060ffdf_1024x681.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Kj9x!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9138c02e-db70-4952-af3b-39a11060ffdf_1024x681.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In 1723, **Peshwa Baji Rao I**, swept through **Malwa** with astonishing speed and precision, flanked by the rising stars of Maratha valor, **Malhar Rao Holkar**,**Ranoji Shinde** and **the Pawar brothers**, each destined to carve their own thrones from the soil of victory.

**Holkar** made **Indore** his seat, while **Shinde** laid the foundation of the **Scindia dynasty** in **Gwalior**, blending martial prowess with cultural patronage. The **Pawars** split their legacy between **Dewas** and **Dhar**, crafting twin kingdoms that echoed with loyalty and lineage.

When the Mughal emperor attempted to take back **Malwa**, he was met with a wall of fire. At **Ajmehra**, **Baji Rao I**, flanked by his brother **Chimaji Appa** and his trusted lieutenants, **Holkar**, **Shinde**, and the **Pawar brothers**, routed the imperial forces with precision and fury. It was not just a defeat for the Mughals, it was a declaration: the Marathas were now the architects of Bharat’s destiny.

With Malwa firmly under control, the Maratha forces surged northward, reaching the **southern fringes of Rajasthan**. But here, Baji Rao’s genius revealed itself not in conquest, but in **calculated restraint**. Unlike earlier Maratha campaigns marked by friction with Rajput states, Baji Rao chose a different path.

He **avoided direct conflict**, respecting Rajput sovereignty and forging **friendly ties**, as he understood that unity among Hindu kingdoms was more vital than short-term spoils.

**Veer Chhatrasal**, the lion of **Bundelkhand**, found himself encircled, his kingdom besieged by **Bangash Khan**, the Mughal general, and the **Rohillas**. With age upon him and allies absent, he turned not to arms, but to **faith and fraternity**.

*"Jo gati bhayi Gajendra ki, wahi gati hamri aaj,  
Baaji jaat Bundel ki, Baaji raakhiyo laaj."*

  
Like **Gajendra**, the elephant king trapped by the crocodile, Chhatrasal invoked **Baji Rao I** as his **Vishnu**, the savior who could descend and deliver.

Moved by the depth of Chhatrasal’s appeal and the righteousness of his cause, Baji Rao rode north with fury and faith. A swift, surgical assault on the Mughal army sent **Bangash fleeing**, his imperial pride in tatters, as Bundelkhand was free again. In deep reverence, **Chhatrasal** restored his throne and offered Baji Rao,a **vast swathe of territory**, including **Jhansi**,and his daughter **Mastani**, born of a **Muslim mistress**, as a gesture of alliance and affection.

While **Baji Rao I** expanded Maratha influence with lightning campaigns and diplomatic finesse, not all within the fold welcomed his rise. **Trimbak Rao Dabhade**, the **Senapati of Chhatrapati Shahu** and scion of the powerful **Dabhade clan**, held a **virtual monopoly over Gujarat**, a region he treated as his hereditary domain. As Baji Rao’s authority grew, especially in revenue collection and military command, **Trimbak Rao felt cornered**, by the Peshwa’s centralizing vision.

  
In response, he forged a **rebellious alliance**,with **other Maratha sardars in Gujarat**,**Bangash Khan**, the Mughal general recently routed by Baji Rao,**Nizam and** even with **Sambhaji II of Kolhapur**, a rival claimant to Maratha legitimacy. The mighty alliance was decisively routed once again by **Peshwa Baji Rao I** at the **Battle of Dabhol**. Among the fallen was **Trimbhak**, whose death marked the collapse of the opposition’s leadership.

In the aftermath, a long-standing internal dispute between **Shahu’s Satara faction** and **Sambhaji II of Kolhapur** was finally resolved through the **Treaty of Warna** in **April 1731**, that formally **demarcated the territories of Satara and Kolhapur**, bringing a measure of unity to the fragmented Maratha polity. Even the **Nizam** , a frequent adversary, was compelled to **promise non-interference** in future Maratha campaigns.

After **Shivaji’s death**, the **Siddis of Janjira**, once a thorn in the side of the Maratha navy, grew in strength, asserting full control over the **Konkan coast**. Their fortified island strongholds, **Janjira and Underi**, became symbols of defiance.

In **1773**, a war of succession erupted among the sons of **Yakut Khan**, the Siddi chief. **Peshwa Baji Rao II**, ever the strategist, backed **Abdul Rehman**, one of the contenders, and brokered a **peace treaty** that split the coast,**Siddis retained Janjira and Underi while Marathas held Raigad and Rewas.**

But peace was short-lived as the Siddis violated the treaty and began raiding **Maratha territories in Konkan**, Baji Rao unleashed his brother, the fierce and brilliant **Chimaji Appa**. In a swift and **surprise assault on Rewas**, Chimaji routed the Siddi forces, forcing them to **sue for peace** once again.

Baji Rao’s final push was to the seat of the Mughal power itself, Delhi, when he sent an army under Malhar Rao Holkar, Pilaji Jadha, as he began the march on November 12, 1736. The Marathas under Holkar marched right up to Delhi, capturing the entire Doab region, and on March 28, 1737 decisively routed the Mughal army at the Battle of Delhi.

The Mughal Emperor’s plea to Nizam and the Nawab of Bhopal was in vain, as the combined forces were once again routed in the Battle of Bhopal on December 24, 1737. Nizam-Ul-Mulk was forced to ceded the entire Malwa to Marathas, and the Mughals had to pay indemnity, a total victory for Baji Rao 1.

Chimaji Appa also ran a succesful campaign against the Portuguese on the West Coast, defeating them in a pitched battle at Vasai, as well as capturing Salsette and Ghoribunder fort.

Finally on April 28, 1740 Baji Rao 1 passed away near Khargone, but he had taken the Maratha Empire to the greatest heights ever.
